topic: A student discovers their teacher follows them on social media
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification pinged, a sharp, unwelcome jolt against the dull monotony of my evening. My thumb hovered over the screen, a slow, deliberate movement. It was Professor Albright. His face, usually a mask of academic detachment, was smiling in a candid photo taken at some outdoor event. A slow, hot burn started low in my stomach, tightening my chest. I zoomed in, scrutinizing the background, searching for any sign of hypocrisy, any crack in his carefully constructed facade. A slow, cruel smile stretched across my lips. I didn't just want to see him; I wanted to dismantle the image, to show everyone the man beneath the tweed jacket. I spent the next hour crafting a series of pointed, subtly barbed comments, each one a tiny, precise shard aimed directly at his perceived failings.

[story 2]

I felt a strange, icy calm settle over me as I scrolled through the feed. It was him. My professor. Posting pictures of his weekend hikes, casual updates about his research. A sudden, intense surge of resentment washed over me, hot and sharp. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles strain beneath my skin. I stared at his latest post, a faint, almost imperceptible tremor running through my hand. It wasn't anger, exactly; it was a deep, simmering need to disrupt. I began drafting messages, carefully worded critiques disguised as intellectual curiosity, designed not just to criticize his work, but to expose the perceived distance between his classroom persona and this public display of personal life. Every keystroke felt weighted, a deliberate act of sabotage.

[story 3]

The phone screen illuminated my face, and the smile on Professor Albright's profile seemed to mock me. A restless energy began to vibrate beneath my skin, a restless need to prove a point, to make him feel the same scrutiny I was applying to him. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I wasn't thinking about grades or assignments anymore. I was thinking about control. Slowly, deliberately, I started curating my own reactions, feeding the algorithm with pointed questions and thinly veiled skepticism. A slow, satisfied smirk played on my lips as I watched the engagement metrics rise. It felt good, this quiet assertion of power, this careful maneuvering in the digital arena. I wanted him to see the cost of his casualness.
